name: Tafsir al-Mizan
author: Allamah Muhammad Husayn Tabatabai
text:
# Ayahs

```arabic
وَلَقَدْ أَرْسَلْنَا مُوسَىٰ بِـَٔايَـٰتِنَا وَسُلْطَـٰنٍ مُّبِينٍ (٩٦)
```

```english
Certainly We sent Moses with Our signs and a manifest authority (96)
```

```arabic
إِلَىٰ فِرْعَوْنَ وَمَلَإِي۟هِۦ فَٱتَّبَعُوٓا۟ أَمْرَ فِرْعَوْنَ ۖ وَمَآ أَمْرُ فِرْعَوْنَ بِرَشِيدٍ (٩٧)
```

```english
to Pharaoh and his elite, but they followed Pharaoh’s dictates, and Pharaoh’s dictates were not right-minded. (97)
```

```arabic
يَقْدُمُ قَوْمَهُۥ يَوْمَ ٱلْقِيَـٰمَةِ فَأَوْرَدَهُمُ ٱلنَّارَ ۖ وَبِئْسَ ٱلْوِرْدُ ٱلْمَوْرُودُ (٩٨)
```

```english
On the Day of Resurrection he will lead his people and conduct them into the Fire: an evil goal for the incoming! (98)
```

```arabic
وَأُتْبِعُوا۟ فِى هَـٰذِهِۦ لَعْنَةً وَيَوْمَ ٱلْقِيَـٰمَةِ ۚ بِئْسَ ٱلرِّفْدُ ٱلْمَرْفُودُ (٩٩)
```

```english
They are pursued by a curse in this [world], as well as on the Day of Resurrection; evil is the award conferred [upon them]! (99)
```

## (Commentary)

This is an allusion to the story of Moses (موسى) — *the one to whom Allah spoke (الكليم)* — peace be upon him (عليه السلام). He is the most frequently mentioned of the prophets in the Qur’an; he is mentioned by name in one hundred and thirty-odd places across some thirty-odd chapters. The Qur’an has paid more attention to the details of his story than any other, yet the Exalted has summarized the account in this chapter, contenting Himself with a brief allusion to it.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **وَلَقَدْ أَرْسَلْنَا مُوسَىٰ بِآيَاتِنَا وَسُلْطَانٍ مُبِينٍ** **"Certainly We sent Moses with Our signs and a manifest authority"** (11:96). The preposition *bi* (ب) in His saying **بِآيَاتِنَا** (with Our signs) denotes accompaniment; that is, We sent Moses accompanied by Our signs. This is because the prophets and messengers whom Allah sent and supported with miraculous signs (الآيات المعجزة) fall into two categories. One category consists of those who were given a miraculous sign according to what their people proposed, such as Salih (صالح), peace be upon him (عليه السلام), who was supported by the sign of the she-camel. The other category consists of those supported by signs at the very beginning of their mission, such as Moses, Jesus, and Muhammad, peace be upon them (عليه السلام). As the Exalted said, addressing Moses (عليه السلام): **اذْهَبْ أَنْتَ وَأَخُوكَ بِآيَاتِي** **"Go, you and your brother, with My signs"** (20:42). Regarding Jesus (عليه السلام), He said: **وَرَسُولًا إِلَىٰ بَنِي إِسْرَائِيلَ أَنِّي قَدْ جِئْتُكُمْ بِآيَةٍ مِنْ رَبِّكُمْ** **"and [as] an apostle to the Children of Israel, [saying]: 'I have certainly brought you a sign from your Lord...'"** (3:49). Regarding Muhammad, may Allah bless him and his family and grant them peace (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم), He said: **هُوَ الَّذِي أَرْسَلَ رَسُولَهُ بِالْهُدَىٰ** **"It is He who has sent His Apostle with guidance"** (61:9). Guidance (الهدى) — *the divine leading toward truth and the straight path* — here refers to the Qur’an, as evidenced by His saying: **ذَٰلِكَ الْكِتَابُ لَا رَيْبَ ۛ فِيهِ ۛ هُدًى لِلْمُتَّقِينَ** **"This is the Book, there is no doubt in it, a guidance to the God-conscious"** (2:2). The Exalted also said: **وَاتَّبَعُوا النُّورَ الَّذِي أُنْزِلَ مَعَهُ** **"and follow the light that has been sent down with him"** (7:157).

Thus, Moses (عليه السلام) was sent with signs and a manifest authority. It is evident that these signs (الآيات) refer to the supernatural phenomena (الأمور الخارقة) that occurred at his hand, as indicated by the context of his stories in the Noble Qur’an.

As for authority (السلطان) — *the decisive proof and conclusive argument that dominates minds and understandings* — it encompasses both the miraculous sign and the rational argument. If taken in this sense, its conjunction with "signs" would be an instance of conjoining the general (العام) to the specific (الخاص).

It is not improbable that being sent with "manifest authority" means that Allah, the Glorified, gave him mastery over the prevailing circumstances between him and the House of Pharaoh — that tyrannical oppressor (الجبار الطاغي) whose like no messenger was tested with except Moses (عليه السلام). Yet Allah the Exalted granted Moses victory over him until He drowned him and his hosts and delivered the Children of Israel by his hand. This meaning is suggested by His saying: **قَالَا رَبَّنَا إِنَّنَا نَخَافُ أَنْ يَفْرُطَ عَلَيْنَا أَوْ أَنْ يَطْغَىٰ قَالَ لَا تَخَافَا ۖ إِنَّنِي مَعَكُمَا أَسْمَعُ وَأَرَىٰ** **"They said, 'Our Lord! We are indeed afraid that he will forestall us or become [more] rebellious.' He said, 'Do not be afraid, for I am indeed with you, and I hear and see'"** (20:45-46), and His saying to Moses (عليه السلام): **لَا تَخَفْ إِنَّكَ أَنْتَ الْأَعْلَىٰ** **"Do not be afraid, for you will indeed have the upper hand"** (20:68).

In this verse and its counterparts, there is a clear indication that the mission of Moses (عليه السلام) was not restricted to his people from the Children of Israel, but rather encompassed them and others.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **إِلَىٰ فِرْعَوْنَ وَمَلَئِهِ فَاتَّبَعُوا أَمْرَ فِرْعَوْنَ ۖ وَمَا أَمْرُ فِرْعَوْنَ بِرَشِيدٍ** **"to Pharaoh and his elite, but they followed Pharaoh’s dictates, and Pharaoh’s dictates were not right-minded"** (11:97). Attributing his mission to Pharaoh and his elite (الملأ) — *the nobles and dignitaries of the people who fill hearts with awe* — rather than all his people, is perhaps to indicate that the commoners were merely followers who had no opinion except what their dignitaries saw for them.

Regarding His saying: **فَاتَّبَعُوا أَمْرَ فِرْعَوْنَ** (but they followed Pharaoh’s dictates), it appears that "dictate" or "command" (الأمر) here is more general than mere speech, encompassing both word and deed. This is similar to what Allah narrated from Pharaoh in his saying: **قَالَ فِرْعَوْنُ مَا أُرِيكُمْ إِلَّا مَا أَرَىٰ وَمَا أَهْدِيكُمْ إِلَّا سَبِيلَ الرَّشَادِ** **"Pharaoh said, 'I only show you what I see [to be right], and I only guide you to the way of rectitude'"** (40:29). Thus, it applies to the tradition (السنة) and the path (الطريقة) that he adopted and commanded. It is as if the verse is a counter-statement to this claim of Pharaoh, whereby Allah the Exalted belied him by saying: **وَمَا أَمْرُ فِرْعَوْنَ بِرَشِيدٍ** (and Pharaoh’s dictates were not right-minded).

The term *rashid* (الرشيد) is a paradigm of *fa'il* derived from *rushd* (الرشد) — *rectitude and right-mindedness* — which is the opposite of error (الغي). That is, Pharaoh’s command was not possessed of rectitude such that it could lead to the truth; rather, it was possessed of error and ignorance. It has also been said that *rashid* means "guiding" (المرشد).

In this sentence — namely, **وَمَا أَمْرُ فِرْعَوْنَ بِرَشِيدٍ** — the manifest noun (الظاهر) is placed where a pronoun (المضمر) would normally be; the original structure would be "his command" (أمره). Perhaps the benefit in this is that the name "Pharaoh" itself serves as evidence for the lack of rectitude in the command, a nuance that could never be conveyed by a pronoun.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **يَقْدُمُ قَوْمَهُ يَوْمَ الْقِيَامَةِ فَأَوْرَدَهُمُ النَّارَ ۖ وَبِئْسَ الْوِرْدُ الْمَوْرُودُ** **"On the Day of Resurrection he will lead his people and conduct them into the Fire: an evil place to be conducted to!"** (11:98). That is, Pharaoh will lead his people because they followed his command; thus, he became an imam for them among the leaders of misguidance. The Exalted said: **وَجَعَلْنَاهُمْ أَئِمَّةً يَدْعُونَ إِلَى النَّارِ** **"We made them leaders who invite to the Fire"** (28:41).

His saying: **فَأَوْرَدَهُمُ النَّارَ** (and conduct them into the Fire) is a consequence of the preceding; meaning, he leads them and thus conducts them into the Fire. The use of the past tense (الماضي) is to denote the certainty of its occurrence (تحقق الوقوع). It has also been suggested that it is a consequence of His saying: **فَاتَّبَعُوا أَمْرَ فِرْعَوْنَ** (but they followed Pharaoh’s dictates); meaning, they followed him, and that following conducted them into the Fire. To support this meaning, some have cited His saying: **وَحَاقَ بِآلِ فِرْعَوْنَ سُوءُ الْعَذَابِ النَّارُ يُعْرَضُونَ عَلَيْهَا غُدُوًّا وَعَشِيًّا ۖ وَيَوْمَ تَقُومُ السَّاعَةُ أَدْخِلُوا آلَ فِرْعَوْنَ أَشَدَّ الْعَذَابِ** **"and a terrible punishment beset Pharaoh’s clan: the Fire, to which they are exposed morning and evening; and on the day when the Hour sets in [it will be said], 'Admit Pharaoh’s clan into the severest punishment!'"** (40:45-46). These verses indicate their punishment from the moment of death before the Day of Resurrection. However, it is not hidden that the verses clearly indicate the opposite of what was argued, as they describe the punishment before the Day of Resurrection as "exposure" (العرض) morning and evening, while on the Day of Resurrection it is "entry" (الدخول) into the severest punishment, which is recorded therein as being the Fire.

Regarding His saying: **وَبِئْسَ الْوِرْدُ الْمَوْرُودُ** (an evil place to be conducted to!), the term *wird* (الورد) refers to the water that thirsty animals or humans approach for drinking. Al-Raghib said in *al-Mufradat*: "The origin of *wurud* (الورود) is the seeking of water, then it is used for other things. It is said: 'I reached (وردت) the water,' and I am the one reaching (وارد), and the water is the place reached (مورود). The camels were brought to the water. He said: **وَلَمَّا وَرَدَ مَاءَ مَدْيَنَ** **'And when he arrived at the well of Midian...'** (28:23). The *wird* is the water prepared for being reached." [End of quote].

Based on this, there is a subtle metaphor (استعارة لطيفة) in the speech, comparing the ultimate goal that a person seeks in life through their exerted efforts to the water sought by a thirsty person. Thus, the sweetness of the happiness that a person seeks through their deeds is a watering place (ورد) they reach. The ultimate happiness of man is the pleasure of Allah and Paradise. However, when they went astray by following Pharaoh’s command and missed the path of true happiness, their goal was transformed into the Fire. Thus, the Fire became the watering place they reach — and an evil watering place it is! For a watering place (الورد) is that which extinguishes the burning of the chest and quenches the thirsty core; it is sweet water and a pleasant, easy-flowing source. But if it is transformed into the punishment of the Fire, then it is an evil watering place to be conducted to.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **وَأُتْبِعُوا فِي هَٰذِهِ لَعْنَةً وَيَوْمَ الْقِيَامَةِ ۚ بِئْسَ الرِّفْدُ الْمَرْفُودُ** **"They were pursued by a curse in this [world], and [they will be] on the Day of Resurrection; evil is the award they will be awarded!"** (11:99). That is, they followed Pharaoh’s command, so they were pursued by a curse (لعنة) from Allah in this world — a distancing from His mercy and an expulsion from the court of His proximity. The manifestation of the curse that pursued them was the drowning, or it is the decree from the Exalted to distance them from mercy, written in the records of their deeds, the effects of which include the drowning and the punishment of the Hereafter.

His saying: **وَيَوْمَ الْقِيَامَةِ ۚ بِئْسَ الرِّفْدُ الْمَرْفُودُ** (and on the Day of Resurrection; evil is the award they will be awarded!). The term *rifd* (الرفد) means a gift (العطية), and its linguistic root means assistance (العون). A gift is called *rifd* or *marfud* because it assists the recipient in their needs. The meaning is: evil is their award on the Day of Resurrection, which is the Fire in which they are fueled. This verse is similar to His saying in another place: **وَأَتْبَعْنَاهُمْ فِي هَٰذِهِ الدُّنْيَا لَعْنَةً ۖ وَيَوْمَ الْقِيَامَةِ هُمْ مِنَ الْمَقْبُوحِينَ** **"We made a curse to follow them in this world, and on the Day of Resurrection they will be among the loathed"** (28:42).

It is also possible to take "the Day of Resurrection" (يَوْمَ الْقِيَامَةِ) as an adverbial qualification (ظرف), making the verse connected to His saying **أُتْبِعُوا** (they were pursued) or to His saying **لَعْنَةً** (a curse), similar to His saying **فِي هَٰذِهِ** (in this [world]). The meaning would then be: Allah pursued them in the world and the Hereafter with a curse, or Allah pursued them with the curse of the world and the Hereafter. Then a new sentence begins: "Evil is the award they will be awarded," referring to the curse that pursued them or the pursuit itself with the curse.

Completed, and praise be to Allah.
